ancestors.notes: Descent from Galician-Portuguese / medieval European Portuguese carried to Brazil from the 16th c.
Old Tupi (lingua geral) substrate: toponyms and everyday lexicon (abacaxi, mandioca, capim, jacare, peteca).
West-Central African (Kimbundu/Kikongo/Yoruba) adstrate from the enslaved population: samba, moleque, cacula, cafune, quilombo; often invoked for open pretonic vowels and syllable-timing tendencies.

name: Brazilian Portuguese

allophone_rules.notes: Word-final ⟨-em / -ém / -êm⟩ is the nasal DIPHTHONG in Brazilian Portuguese too — bem [ˈbẽj̃], quem [ˈkẽj̃], também [tãˈbẽj̃], homem [ˈomẽj̃], ontem [ˈõtẽj̃] (Barbosa & Albano 2004: 228; Câmara Jr. 1970 §III; Mateus & d'Andrade 2000: ch.4). Unlike European Portuguese, BP does NOT centralise the nucleus to [ɐ̃]: it keeps the close-mid nasal [ẽ] and adds a nasal palatal offglide [j̃], so [̃]→[̃j̃] with the mid front nucleus left intact (hence no BR analogue of PT_FINAL_EM_NUCLEUS). Gated on the ORIGINAL preceding nucleus being a mid/high front vowel so ⟨-im -om -um⟩ (sim, bom, um) keep a plain nasal monophthong; word-final grapheme ⟨m⟩ only, so an ⟨em⟩ before a consonant (tempo, sempre) and word-final ⟨-en⟩ are excluded.

Word-final ⟨-am⟩ (3rd-person-plural verb ending) is the nasal diphthong [ɐ̃w̃] in Brazilian Portuguese — falam [ˈfalɐ̃w̃], custam [ˈkustɐ̃w̃], brilham [ˈbɾiʎɐ̃w̃] (Barbosa & Albano 2004: 228; Câmara Jr. 1970 §III). The nucleus [ɐ̃] is supplied by the ⟨a⟩→[ɐ] nasal raise (PT_NASAL_A_RAISE) and this rule adds the [w̃] offglide to the coda ⟨m⟩ tilde: [̃]→[̃w̃]. Gated on the ORIGINAL preceding nucleus being low [a ɐ], disjoint from the ⟨-em⟩ glide; word-final ⟨am⟩ only occurs as this verb ending, and an ⟨am⟩ before a consonant (campo, ambos) is not word-final and keeps the plain nasal [ɐ̃].

Affrication of /t/ before a RAISED final unstressed vowel (leite [ˈlejt͡ʃi], gente [ˈʒẽt͡ʃi], sete [ˈsɛt͡ʃi]; Barbosa & Albano 2004: 228; Câmara Jr. 1970 §III). The pre-lexical positional map affricates /t d/ only before a GRAPHEME ⟨i⟩ (before_i → tia [ˈt͡ʃiɐ]); it cannot reach the case where the trigger [i] is a word-final unstressed ⟨e⟩ that only becomes [i] post-lexically (positional selects the near-close [ɪ], raised to [i] by BR_RAISE_FINAL_E). This rule closes that feeding gap by conditioning on the FOLLOWING slot's underlying high front vowel — the near-close [ɪ] (the pre-raise realisation the rescorer reads on the neighbour) or a lexical [i] — so a ⟨t⟩ before it affricates regardless of whether the trigger was spelled ⟨i⟩ or a raised ⟨e⟩. It is a no-op where the positional map already produced [t͡ʃ] (the slot phoneme is no longer /t/). The non-palatalising nordeste (recife/norte/ce) and the conservative-dental caipira/sul/pt-UY varieties disable this rule by id.

Voiced counterpart of BR_AFFRIC_T_RAISED: /d/ affricates to [d͡ʒ] before a raised final unstressed vowel (tarde [ˈtaɾd͡ʒi], onde [ˈõd͡ʒi], pode [ˈpɔd͡ʒi]; Barbosa & Albano 2004: 228). Closes the same feeding gap where the trigger [i] is a word-final ⟨e⟩ raised only post-lexically (positional [ɪ] → [i] via BR_RAISE_FINAL_O/E), which the grapheme-⟨i⟩ positional before_i rule cannot see. No-op where the positional map already produced [d͡ʒ]. Disabled by id in the non-palatalising nordeste and conservative-dental caipira/sul/pt-UY varieties.

Coda-conditioned vowel nasalisation, general Portuguese, retained in Brazilian Portuguese (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000: ch.2, 'Nasality'; Barbosa & Albano 2004; Câmara Jr. 1970 §III): a vowel followed by a tautosyllabic (coda) nasal ⟨m/n⟩ nasalises and the nasal consonant is absorbed (campo, canto, ambos). The nasalisation is modelled pre-lexically by the positional 'm'/'n' before_consonant/word_final → [̃] (U+0303 combining tilde) map. This rule supplies only the VOWEL-QUALITY shift for ⟨a⟩: BP nasal /a/ is the raised central [ɐ̃] (campo [ˈkɐ̃pu]), so the oral [a] selected by the positional map raises to the ORAL base [ɐ] before the tilde — [ɐ]+[̃] = [ɐ̃]. The tilde comes only from the m/n slot, so no vowel is double-tilded. Fires ONLY when the next slot is the tilde, so an INTERVOCALIC (onset) ⟨m/n⟩ leaves the vowel oral (cama [ˈkamɐ], ano [ˈanu]).

Vowel-quality companion of the coda-nasalisation (see PT_NASAL_A_RAISE; Mateus & d'Andrade 2000: ch.2). BP nasal /e/ is the close-mid [ẽ], so the open [ɛ] raises to the oral base [e] before the nasalisation tilde — [e]+[̃] = [ẽ] (tempo [ˈtẽpu], sempre). Surface is oral [e]; the tilde is supplied only by the coda m/n slot (no double tilde). Fires only before the tilde, so an oral ⟨e⟩ before an onset ⟨m/n⟩ (nome, pena) is unchanged.

Vowel-quality companion of the coda-nasalisation (see PT_NASAL_A_RAISE; Mateus & d'Andrade 2000: ch.2). BP nasal /o/ is the close-mid [õ], so the open [ɔ] raises to the oral base [o] before the nasalisation tilde — [o]+[̃] = [õ] (bom [ˈbõ], onde). Surface is oral [o]; the tilde is supplied only by the coda m/n slot (no double tilde). Fires only before the tilde, so an oral ⟨o⟩ before an onset ⟨m/n⟩ (fome, sono) is unchanged. High vowels ⟨i u⟩ need no companion rule — [ĩ ũ] share the oral base [i u] (sim [ˈsĩ], mundo [ˈmũdu]).

Final unstressed vowel raising in the ⟨-es⟩ ending (Barbosa & Albano 2004: 229; Câmara Jr. 1970 §III): an unstressed word-final ⟨e⟩ raises to close [i] before the plural/2sg coda ⟨-s⟩ exactly as it does word-finally without the ⟨s⟩ (dente [ˈdẽt͡ʃi] : dentes [ˈdẽt͡ʃis]; pote : potes; estes [ˈest͡ʃis]; pires [ˈpiɾis]). The bare-final case is BR_RAISE_FINAL_E; here the ⟨s⟩ intervenes so the nucleus is not word-final and the pre-lexical map leaves it [e] (posttonic nucleus_unstressed), which this rule raises. Gated `unstressed` so a stressed ⟨-ês/-és⟩ monosyllable keeps its mid vowel (três [ˈtɾes], mês [ˈmes], dez [ˈdɛs] — the last is [ɛ] and untargeted anyway). `followed_by_2 = word_boundary` restricts it to the true ending, so a medial ⟨e⟩ before a tautosyllabic ⟨s⟩+C (vespa, festa) is untouched. Feeds the /t d/ affrication (BR_AFFRIC_*_RAISED) on the preceding slot, which is why the pt-BR spec runs the allophone pass twice (allophone_passes): the affrication reads this rule's raised [i] only on the second pass.

Voiced-vowel counterpart of BR_RAISE_FINAL_ES: an unstressed word-final ⟨o⟩ raises to close [u] before the plural coda ⟨-s⟩ (gato [ˈɡatu] : gatos [ˈɡatus]; tios [ˈt͡ʃius]; montes/pontos). The bare-final case is BR_RAISE_FINAL_O; here the ⟨s⟩ intervenes so the pre-lexical map leaves the nucleus [o] (posttonic), which this rule raises. Gated `unstressed` so a stressed ⟨-ós/-ôs⟩ (nós [ˈnɔs], avós, pôs) keeps its mid vowel; `followed_by_2 = word_boundary` restricts it to the ending so a medial ⟨o⟩ before ⟨s⟩+C (mosca, gosto) is untouched.

Final unstressed vowel raising (Barbosa & Albano 2004: 229; Câmara Jr. 1970 §III): word-final unstressed /e/ raises to close [i] (leite → [ˈlejt͡ʃi], dente → [ˈdẽt͡ʃi]). The pre-lexical map selects the near-close [ɪ] word-finally; this pass realises the standard BP close [i]. Targeting only the reduced [ɪ] (not underlying /e/) leaves conservative dialects that retain a final [e] untouched, so the delta stays inheritable.

Final unstressed vowel raising (Barbosa & Albano 2004: 229; Câmara Jr. 1970 §III): word-final unstressed /o/ raises to close [u] (gato → [ˈɡatu]). Pretonic reduction is markedly weaker in BP than EP and is not modelled: BP keeps distinct pretonic /e o/, so only the final position raises. Targets the reduced [ʊ] only, leaving conservative dialects that retain a final [o] untouched.

Lexically CLOSE stressed ⟨o⟩ = [o] for this curated set of frequent lexemes, overriding the pre-lexical default (which is the open [ɔ] in the European standard and, by inheritance, ambiguous elsewhere). Portuguese stressed mid-vowel height is lexically/etymologically fixed and not predictable from spelling — ⟨sopa⟩ [ˈsopɐ] (close, < Gmc *suppa) vs ⟨sova⟩ [ˈsɔvɐ] (open) are spelled and stressed alike (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000: ch.3 'The vowels'; Vigário 2003). This rule pins ONLY the stressed-vowel height and lets reduction, rhotics, sandhi and affrication run normally, so it inherits cleanly to every pt-PT / pt-BR dialect child. Includes the ⟨-oso⟩ masculine adjectival suffix (saboroso, gostoso, famoso), which is regularly close [o] against its open [ɔ] feminine ⟨-osa⟩ (PT_LEX_OPEN_O). SEED LIST — the arbitration-confirmed frequent lexemes; the long tail of Portuguese mid-vowel lexemes is documented residual, to be extended from Mateus & d'Andrade / a pronouncing dictionary.

Lexically CLOSE stressed ⟨e⟩ = [e] for this curated set, overriding the pre-lexical default (open [ɛ] first in the European standard). Stressed mid-vowel height is a lexical fact in Portuguese (⟨fresco⟩ [ˈfɾe(ʃ)ku] close vs many open ⟨e⟩ lexemes spelled alike; Mateus & d'Andrade 2000: ch.3; Vigário 2003). Pins only the vowel height, inherits cleanly. SEED LIST — documented residual for the tail.

Lexically OPEN stressed ⟨o⟩ = [ɔ] for this curated set, overriding the pre-lexical default (which is the close [o] first selected in Brazilian and non-reducing inheritors, giving the wrong height for these lexemes). Height is a lexical/morphological fact (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000: ch.3; Vigário 2003): the 3sg present of ⟨-ar/-er⟩ verbs opens the stressed root vowel (mora, corre, espera), and the ⟨-osa⟩ feminine adjectival suffix is open [ɔ] (famosa, gostosa) against its close [o] masculine ⟨-oso⟩ (PT_LEX_CLOSE_O). This rule pins ONLY the stressed-vowel height (reduction, rhotics, l-vocalisation, affrication run normally), so it inherits cleanly to every pt-PT / pt-BR dialect child. SEED LIST — arbitration-confirmed lexemes; the tail is documented residual.

Lexically OPEN stressed ⟨e⟩ = [ɛ] for this curated set, overriding the pre-lexical default where a close [e] is selected (Brazilian and non-reducing inheritors). Includes items whose open height is etymological (Latin short ĕ: terra, serra), morphological (3sg present espera; the copula era) or simply lexical (dez). Mateus & d'Andrade 2000: ch.3; Vigário 2003. Pins only the vowel height and inherits cleanly. SEED LIST — documented residual for the tail.

The ⟨x⟩ of the trazer preterite/subjunctive stem ⟨troux-⟩ is [s], not the default [ʃ] (trouxe [ˈtɾo(w)sɨ] / BP [ˈtɾosi]; Cunha & Cintra 1984, §on ⟨x⟩; the value of orthographic ⟨x⟩ is lexically unpredictable — [ʃ] peixe, [s] trouxe/próximo, [ks] fixo, [z] exame). Keyed on the lexeme because no phonological context separates the trouxe ⟨x⟩=[s] from the luxo ⟨x⟩=[ʃ]; gated on grapheme ⟨x⟩ so it never touches a ⟨ch⟩ or lexical [ʃ]. Pins only the consonant, so vowel reduction and the surrounding processes run normally and it inherits to every pt child. SEED — the broader ⟨x⟩=[s]/[ks]/[z] lexicon (próximo, máximo, fixo, exame) is documented residual.

notes: Brazilian Portuguese. Based directly on late Old Portuguese diaspora phonology. Key innovations: (1) Loss of coda R (ɾ→∅; later urban ɦ/ʁ in some dialects). (2) Coda sibilants remain alveolar (s, z), no universal palato-alveolar outcome. (3) L-vocalisation (l→w) strong and categorical in coda. (4) Palatalisation of /t, d/ > [t͡ʃ, d͡ʒ] before /i/. (5) Much weaker vowel reduction; loss of ɨ. (6) Final /e/→/i/ and /o/→/u/. (7) /rr/ always uvular. (8) Nasal vowel system preserved but with characteristic BR realisation. POST-LEXICAL ALLOPHONY (allophone_rules, base for the BR dialect specs via OVERLAY_BY_ID): final unstressed vowel raising — the near-close [ɪ]/[ʊ] selected word-finally by the positional map is realised as the standard BP close [i]/[u] (gato→[ˈɡatu], leite→[ˈlejt͡ʃi]). Targeting the reduced [ɪ]/[ʊ] (not underlying /e o/) leaves conservative dialects that retain a final [e]/[o] untouched, so the rule inherits cleanly. Deliberately kept in the positional map, NOT duplicated as allophone_rules to avoid double-application: /t d/ affrication before front vowels (grapheme-⟨i⟩ handled by the before_i positional rule) and coda /l/ vocalisation (l→w). The phoneme-conditioned refinement of affrication before a raised final ⟨e⟩ (dente→[ˈdẽt͡ʃi]) is left to the dialect-delta wave, where the non-palatalising nordeste varieties (recife/norte/ce) can opt out by rule id — expressing it in the base would silently re-impose affrication on those inheritors. Deliberately NOT modelled in the base: strong EP-style pretonic reduction (BP keeps distinct pretonic /e o/); coda /s/→[ʃ] chiado (a carioca/RJ dialect delta, not general BP — base stays alveolar [s]/[z]); coda /r/→[h~x] weak fricative (highly variable and, since the broad wikipron pt-BR gold transcribes coda /r/ as [ʁ], adding [h~x] regresses the only pt-BR gold — held as a dialect delta rather than shipped in the base).

sources.author: Barbosa, P.A. & Albano, E.C.
sources.title: Brazilian Portuguese (Illustrations of the IPA)
sources.publisher: Journal of the International Phonetic Association 34(2)
sources.notes: Canonical BP IPA illustration. Documents final unstressed vowel raising /e o/ → [i u] (source for the BR_RAISE_FINAL_E/O allophone rules) and /t d/ affrication before [i] (modelled pre-lexically in positional_graphemes).

sources.author: Câmara Jr., J. Mattoso
sources.title: Estrutura da Língua Portuguesa
sources.notes: Classic structural description of BP phonology: neutralisation of final unstressed vowels to the raised [i]/[u]/[ɐ] and the palatalisation of dental stops before [i].

sources.author: Callou, D. & Leite, Y.
sources.title: Iniciação à fonética e à fonologia (8th ed.)

sources.author: Vigário, Marina
sources.title: The Prosodic Word in European Portuguese
sources.notes: Prosodic-word structure and stress in European Portuguese; background for the stress-conditioned mid-vowel height and reduction facts.

stress.notes: Acordo Ortográfico 1990 conventions: paroxytone default; written accents override; final -i/-u/-r/-l/-z/-x/-im/-om/-um/-ns and nasal diphthongs attract final stress. Unmarked -em/-am stay paroxytone (homem, falam). DIPHTHONGS: declared so the bundled syllabifier counts nuclei correctly — without them every vowel run merges into one nucleus and co-e-lho is read as coe-lho, one syllable short, putting the stress and its vowel reduction on the wrong vowel. ⟨oe⟩ is hiatus, not a diphthong (Cunha & Cintra, §Ditongos).

location.notes: Brazil's representative point. A national variety spanning a continent: weak — but Glottolog placed Brazilian Portuguese in PORTUGAL, which is worse than weak. [Wikidata Q155 P625]
